title: Asthma Phenotyping & Biologic Selection
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Pulmonology Fellows
objectives: Distinguish T2-high from T2-low asthma using biomarkers
Identify the major asthma phenotypes: allergic, eosinophilic, AERD, obesity-related
Interpret blood eosinophils, FeNO, and IgE in asthma phenotyping
Apply GINA 2024 severe asthma treatment algorithm
Select appropriate biologic based on phenotype and comorbidities

guidelineReferences.name: GINA 2024: Difficult-to-Treat and Severe Asthma
guidelineReferences.citation: Global Initiative for Asthma. Difficult-to-Treat and Severe Asthma. 2024
keyPoints: Phenotype with blood eos ≥150, FeNO ≥20, allergen sensitivity
T2-high: eligible for biologics; T2-low: limited options
Match biologic to phenotype and comorbidities

vignette.text:
A 42-year-old woman with asthma since childhood presents for severe asthma evaluation. Despite fluticasone/salmeterol 500/50 BID and montelukast, she has had 3 ED visits and 2 prednisone bursts in the past year. She also has chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps and had a respiratory reaction (wheezing, nasal congestion) after taking ibuprofen 5 years ago.

Past Medical History: Asthma (since age 8), Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (3 surgeries), GERD, Eczema in childhood (resolved)

Medications: Fluticasone/salmeterol 500/50 BID, Montelukast 10mg daily, Albuterol PRN

Allergies: Ibuprofen (respiratory reaction), Dust mites and cats on skin testing

physicalExam.HEENT: Nasal polyps visible bilaterally, boggy turbinates

questions.stem: This patient has asthma with elevated eosinophils, elevated FeNO, nasal polyps, and NSAID sensitivity.
questions.question: What are the major asthma phenotypes and which does this patient have?
questions.hint: Consider T2-high vs T2-low and specific clinical features.

options.text: Phenotypes: Allergic, Eosinophilic, AERD, Obesity-related, T2-low; This patient has Eosinophilic asthma with AERD
Phenotypes: Mild, Moderate, Severe; This patient has Severe asthma
Phenotypes: Childhood-onset, Adult-onset; This patient has Childhood-onset

questions.explanation: Major phenotypes: (1) Allergic (T2-high): elevated IgE, positive allergen testing; (2) Eosinophilic (T2-high): elevated eosinophils ± FeNO; (3) AERD: asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID sensitivity (always eosinophilic); (4) Obesity-related: often T2-low; (5) T2-low: neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic. This patient has AERD (classic triad) within the eosinophilic phenotype.

whyChain.answer: Phenotyping guides biologic selection. T2-high patients are candidates for anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4Rα, or anti-TSLP biologics. T2-low patients have limited biologic options.

questions.stem: You are interpreting this patient's biomarkers: eosinophils 450, FeNO 45 ppb, IgE 385.
questions.question: What do these biomarkers indicate about her asthma endotype?
questions.hint: Consider T2-high thresholds for each biomarker.

options.text: T2-high asthma: eosinophils ≥150, FeNO ≥25, IgE elevated - excellent biologic candidate
T2-low asthma: these biomarkers are all normal
Allergic only: IgE is the only relevant marker

questions.explanation: T2-high biomarker thresholds: Blood eos ≥150-300 cells/μL; FeNO ≥25 ppb; Elevated IgE + positive allergen testing. She has eos 450, FeNO 45, IgE 385 - definitively T2-high. Higher eosinophils predict better response to anti-IL5 biologics.

whyChain.why: What do different eosinophil levels predict?
whyChain.answer: ≥150 = T2-high, eligible for biologics. ≥300 = greater predicted response. ≥500 = excellent predicted response. Her 450 is in the 'very good responder' range.

questions.stem: This patient has asthma, nasal polyps, and a history of respiratory reaction to ibuprofen.
questions.question: What is AERD and what is the underlying mechanism?
questions.hint: Consider the classic triad and COX pathway abnormality.

options.text: Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease: asthma + nasal polyps + NSAID-induced respiratory reactions; caused by COX-1 inhibition shunting arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway

questions.explanation: AERD (formerly Samter's Triad): (1) Asthma (typically severe, eosinophilic), (2) Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, (3) Respiratory reactions to COX-1 inhibitors. NOT IgE-mediated allergy. Mechanism: dysregulated arachidonic acid metabolism with leukotriene overproduction. Affects ~7% of asthmatics, ~15% of severe asthmatics.

whyChain.why: Why do NSAIDs cause reactions if it's not allergy?
whyChain.answer: COX-1 inhibition blocks prostaglandins but not 5-lipoxygenase. In AERD, arachidonic acid shunts to leukotriene pathway causing bronchoconstriction. COX-2 selective inhibitors (celecoxib) are generally safe.
After initial reaction, continued daily aspirin leads to desensitization. High-dose aspirin (325-650mg BID) improves asthma, reduces polyp recurrence. Can be done with biologic therapy.

questions.stem: Before labeling this patient as having 'severe asthma,' you need to confirm the diagnosis.
questions.question: What must be done before diagnosing true severe asthma?

options.text: Confirm diagnosis (spirometry), optimize adherence/technique, treat comorbidities, ensure maximal controller therapy

questions.explanation: Per GINA: 'Difficult-to-treat' = uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA. 'Severe' = remains uncontrolled AFTER addressing: (1) Diagnosis confirmation (spirometry), (2) Adherence, (3) Inhaler technique, (4) Comorbidities (GERD, rhinosinusitis, obesity), (5) Trigger avoidance. Only ~3-10% have true severe asthma.

whyChain.answer: Up to 50% of 'difficult' asthma is poor adherence, incorrect technique, or untreated comorbidities. These patients won't respond to biologics. Fixing modifiable factors may control asthma without expensive biologics.

questions.stem: This patient has confirmed severe eosinophilic asthma with AERD.
questions.question: What are the FDA-approved biologic classes for severe asthma?
questions.hint: Consider the different targets in the T2 pathway.

options.text: Anti-IgE (omalizumab), Anti-IL5/IL5R (mepolizumab, benralizumab, reslizumab), Anti-IL4Rα (dupilumab), Anti-TSLP (tezepelumab)

questions.explanation: FDA-approved biologics: (1) Anti-IgE: Omalizumab; (2) Anti-IL5: Mepolizumab, Reslizumab; Anti-IL5Rα: Benralizumab; (3) Anti-IL4Rα: Dupilumab; (4) Anti-TSLP: Tezepelumab. Each targets different points in T2 inflammation.

whyChain.answer: Omalizumab: binds IgE (allergic pathway). Anti-IL5: reduces eosinophils. Dupilumab: blocks IL-4/IL-13 (IgE, mucus, remodeling). Tezepelumab: blocks TSLP (upstream, works in T2-high and some T2-low).

questions.stem: This patient has eosinophilic asthma with AERD (asthma, polyps, NSAID sensitivity), eosinophils 450, FeNO 45, IgE 385.
questions.question: Which biologic would be MOST appropriate for this patient?
questions.hint: Consider which biologic addresses both her asthma and nasal polyps.

options.text: Dupilumab - addresses both severe asthma AND nasal polyps; effective for eosinophilic disease

questions.explanation: Dupilumab (anti-IL4Rα) is ideal because: (1) FDA-approved for BOTH severe eosinophilic asthma AND CRSwNP; (2) Highly effective for AERD; (3) IL-4/IL-13 blockade addresses eosinophilic inflammation. LIBERTY ASTHMA trials show 70% reduction in exacerbations. SINUS trials show polyp shrinkage. One drug treats both conditions.

whyChain.why: Why not omalizumab if she has elevated IgE?
whyChain.answer: Omalizumab could work for asthma but isn't FDA-approved for polyps. She'd need two treatments. Dupilumab treats both. Her disease is primarily eosinophilic (AERD), which responds better to anti-IL5 or dupilumab.

Anti-IL5 agents are excellent alternatives. However, dupilumab uniquely treats nasal polyps (FDA approved). Mepolizumab is FDA-approved for EGPA, which should be ruled out if eosinophils are very high.

questions.stem: You are comparing biologics for different patient profiles.
questions.question: Which biologic-patient matching is CORRECT?

options.text: High IgE + allergen-driven → Omalizumab; High eos without allergy → Mepolizumab/Benralizumab; Asthma + polyps → Dupilumab; T2-low → Tezepelumab
All patients should start with omalizumab

questions.explanation: Matching: (1) Allergic (high IgE, allergens) → Omalizumab; (2) Eosinophilic (high eos) → Mepolizumab, Benralizumab, Reslizumab; (3) Asthma + polyps/eczema → Dupilumab; (4) T2-low or uncertain → Tezepelumab (most upstream).

whyChain.answer: Choose biologic addressing most conditions. Asthma + polyps + eczema → Dupilumab (approved for all three). Asthma + food allergy → Omalizumab (approved for both).

questions.stem: You are explaining dupilumab to this patient.
questions.question: What are the key features of dupilumab for severe asthma?
questions.hint: Consider mechanism, dosing, and side effects.

options.text: Anti-IL4Rα blocking IL-4 and IL-13; 200-300mg SC every 2 weeks; also approved for atopic dermatitis, CRSwNP, EoE; side effects include injection site reaction, transient hypereosinophilia
Given IV monthly; only for allergic asthma

questions.explanation: Dupilumab: Monoclonal antibody blocking IL-4 receptor alpha. Dose: 200mg or 300mg SC every 2 weeks. FDA approved for: severe eosinophilic asthma, atopic dermatitis, CRSwNP, EoE, prurigo nodularis. Side effects: injection site reactions, conjunctivitis (especially AD), transient hypereosinophilia.

whyChain.answer: Dupilumab can cause temporary increase in blood eosinophils. IL-4/IL-13 blockade reduces eosinophil migration into tissues, causing blood accumulation. Usually resolves. Monitor but don't stop drug. Rarely can unmask EGPA.

questions.stem: The patient asks about anti-IL5 options as alternatives.
questions.question: What are the differences between mepolizumab, benralizumab, and reslizumab?

options.text: Mepolizumab/reslizumab bind IL-5; Benralizumab binds IL-5 receptor and depletes eosinophils via ADCC; Mepolizumab SC monthly, Benralizumab SC q8 weeks, Reslizumab IV monthly

questions.explanation: Anti-IL5 pathway: (1) Mepolizumab: anti-IL5, 100mg SC q4 weeks, also approved for EGPA, HES; (2) Benralizumab: anti-IL5Rα, depletes eosinophils via ADCC, 30mg SC q4 weeks x3 then q8 weeks; (3) Reslizumab: anti-IL5, weight-based IV q4 weeks.

whyChain.answer: Benralizumab depletes to near-zero (ADCC). Mepolizumab reduces but doesn't deplete. Clinical efficacy similar. Benralizumab's q8 week dosing may be convenient.

questions.stem: This patient has elevated eosinophils. You want to rule out other conditions.
questions.question: What eosinophilic conditions should be considered in severe asthma?

options.text: ABPA (Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis) and EGPA (Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis) - both can mimic or coexist with severe asthma

questions.explanation: Important overlaps: (1) ABPA: asthma + Aspergillus sensitization, IgE often >1000, positive Aspergillus IgE/IgG, central bronchiectasis; (2) EGPA: asthma + very high eos (>1500) + systemic symptoms (neuropathy, skin, cardiac). Screen with ANCA if eos very high. This patient's negative Aspergillus IgE makes ABPA unlikely.

whyChain.answer: Criteria: Asthma, elevated IgE (>1000), positive Aspergillus skin test or IgE, elevated Aspergillus IgG, CT findings. Treatment: steroids ± itraconazole.
Red flags: very high eos (>1500), peripheral neuropathy, purpura, cardiac involvement. ANCA positive in ~40%. Mepolizumab is FDA-approved for EGPA.

questions.stem: Integrating all information about this patient.
questions.question: What is the complete assessment and management plan?
questions.hint: Connect phenotype, biomarkers, comorbidities, and treatment.

options.text: Severe eosinophilic asthma with AERD (T2-high), confirmed by elevated eos/FeNO, polyps, NSAID sensitivity; after confirming adherence, start dupilumab for asthma and polyps; consider aspirin desensitization
Mild allergic asthma, continue current therapy

questions.explanation: Complete synthesis: (1) Phenotype: Severe eosinophilic asthma with AERD, T2-high; (2) ABPA ruled out (negative Aspergillus IgE); (3) Treatment: Dupilumab - FDA approved for asthma AND CRSwNP; (4) Adjunct: Consider aspirin desensitization; (5) Goals: Reduce exacerbations, eliminate OCS, improve polyps.

whyChain.answer: With dupilumab: ~70% reduction in exacerbations, improved FEV1, reduced OCS, improved polyp scores. Full benefit may take 6-12 months. Polyp benefit often seen within 6-8 weeks.
